data.title: The Rise of Autonomous Drone Warfare
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Five years ago, the idea of launching 1,000 drones in a single night would have sounded like science fiction. Today, it is reality over Ukraine. Russia has industrialized drone warfare at extraordinary scale, mass-producing cheap, autonomous attack drones and launching them in relentless waves against Ukrainian cities and infrastructure.

“I think people had completely forgotten what a high-intensity war looks like, but the Ukraine war is high-intensity in every conceivable way,” Robert Tollast, research fellow on the land warfare team at RUSI (U.K.), said. “[It] has given us a scale that people just couldn’t imagine … and completely caught the whole world off-guard.”

Russian forces first deployed Iranian-supplied Shahed-136 suicide drones in September 2022, seven months after invading Ukraine, before manufacturing the copycat Geran-2. Since then, they have used layered-strike tactics, sending swarms of decoy drones to prematurely exhaust Ukraine’s defenses and clear a path for attack drones.

But now, Ukraine has responded with its interceptor drones. Quick to manufacture—often 3D-printed—these small, high-speed UAVs carry explosive payloads and will track, target, and literally crash into a Geran or Shahed drone and explode to bring it down. They are also a fraction of the cost of these already-cheap attack drones.

“We knew we needed a disruptive technology,” said Andrii Hrytseniuk, head of Brave1, Ukraine’s Ministry of Digital Transformation. “We couldn’t compete with Russia on the scale of resources, and we had a lack of air defense missiles; they were just too expensive and a lot more expensive than a Shahed. So with Ukrainian manufacturers and our Armed Forces, we started to [develop and] grow interceptors.”

AI has become a big part of Ukraine’s interceptor drone development. Early interceptors were manually controlled, but Russian maneuvering threats and electronic warfare prompted Ukrainian engineers to integrate AI-assisted guidance so the UAVs could lock on and track Shahed-type targets, even in jammed environments.

For example, “Bagnet,” manufactured by Tenebris, uses optical sensors and on-board machine vision to detect and intercept targets without relying on GPS or radio links. Similarly, “UEB-1” from Osiris AI uses predictive algorithms to track maneuvering Shahed-type drones, while Wild Hornets’ “Sting” employs AI for fast autonomous tracking during its final intercept, at speeds exceeding 300 km/h.

But Russia’s attack drones have also evolved to keep pace with Ukraine’s interceptor UAVs. “Right now, Russia is on its fifth version of its Geran drone—they’ve been changing engines, increasing speed, increasing [evasive] maneuvers, adding more missiles on top,” Hrytseniuk said. “But we are ready and are always working in advance on counter solutions as we understand what we are doing … and artificial intelligence is crucial.”

Drone wars

The co-evolution of Shahed-type UAVs and cheap, AI-equipped interceptor drones is reshaping warfare far beyond Ukraine. Having watched the drone war unfold over Ukrainian skies, nations across the West have been rapidly developing their own, relatively cheap interceptor UAVs while ramping manufacturing capacity, often in partnership with Ukrainian companies.

“We’re only just waking up in the West to the fact that we might all be on the war footing … and Ukraine really has an edge here,” RUSI’s Tollast said.

“Many Western defense companies now have got partnerships with Ukrainians,” he added. “They’ve got offices in Ukraine, they’ve got Ukrainian partner companies—or, more critically, they are repeatedly testing their interceptor drones in Ukraine.”

A case in point is the Merops counter-drone system, developed by California-based Perennial Autonomy, previously known as Project Eagle and backed by former Google CEO Eric Schmidt. The system launches its “Surveyor” interceptor drones that can be remotely piloted or operate autonomously if communication links are jammed. First used by Ukrainian forces around June 2024, reports indicate that Surveyors have intercepted more than 1,000 Shahed-type drones in Ukraine, and Merops has also been deployed at Polish and Romanian borders and across the Middle East.

Meanwhile, Brave1 is continuously forging partnerships across the European Union and NATO to drive the development and manufacture of interceptor drones forward. As part of a tech-sharing agreement, the U.K. and Ukraine are jointly manufacturing the Ukraine-designed interceptor drone, Project Octopus, with a target to produce thousands every month. “More than 25 companies now have a license to produce Octopuses,” Hrytseniuk said.

In a collaboration with U.S. software and data analytics company Palantir, the Brave1 Dataroom also recently launched, which is using battlefield data to test and train new AI models for military applications. Its initial focus is to develop autonomous tech to detect and intercept UAVs. “Who has the biggest volume of data in the world about Shahed, Gerbera [decoy] drones, and other weapons? Ukraine,” Hrytseniuk said. “Ukrainian and international companies from allied countries can build products and train them using this data.”

Software focus

Founded in 2023, Munich-based interceptor drone manufacturer Tytan Technologies has focused on developing sophisticated AI software for low-cost UAVs from the word “go.” Its co-founder and CTO, Batuhan Yumurtacı, was named in this year’s Forbes 30 Under 30 AI list. Working closely with German and Ukrainian armed forces, the company claims that its one-way UAVs can detect, track, and destroy hostile drones at “up to 100× cheaper than conventional solutions.”

“We’ve tried to push a paradigm change where we make cheap and simple hardware … and then enhance it with very sophisticated AI-powered software that can detect and neutralize the target,” Max Enders, Tytan’s head of business development and government affairs, said.

Tytan’s interceptors can be either 3D-printed or manufactured with off-the-shelf components. According to Enders, the “Metis” carries a 1-kg payload to detonate the target in the air, while a smaller interceptor is designed to destroy a target on impact. He also highlighted how, in the future, the company will manufacture a type of hybrid-rocket interceptor that will reach “enormous speeds” and still operate on its existing software.

“The idea is that we want these systems to be so cheap that they’re like ammunition,” Enders said. “You want them to be used en masse by soldiers, not to be these gold-plated solutions where you only have a couple of them and you can’t produce enough.”

Tytan’s command-and-control software is designed to integrate with existing layered air-defense networks used by, say, the Ukrainian, German, or Polish Armed Forces and can combine radar and sensors, command systems, and the inceptor drones into a unified defense architecture to detect, track, and neutralize attack drones. The interceptor drones themselves carry optical and thermal cameras, allowing the software to use sensor fusion to analyze incoming targets against its database.

“Is it a Shahed, is it a Gerbera drone, or is it an ISR [intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance] drone?” Enders said. “The software will classify the drone and then make recommendations as to the use of [defense] effector to be used [in retaliation].”

As Enders explained, if the operator decides to deploy an interceptor drone, this can be guided toward the incoming attack UAV. The system’s AI computer vision model can then be used to detect and lock the interceptor onto the target. “The human operator can then take the decision to fly into the target at speed and take it out,” Enders said.

Autonomous drone swarming is also on Tytan’s radar: The company is working on software so that groups of interceptors can communicate to classify and intercept targets “communally.” “For Russia, [autonomous swarming] could defeat the purpose of having simple, cheap drones that can be produced in mass,” Enders said. “But we see this from China, and we watch very closely what the Chinese drone industry is doing.”

Backed by some $49 million in funds, Tytan is now ramping drone production at its facility in Bavaria, Germany. Right now, the company is producing about 140 drones a month, but according to Enders, by July, operational capability will reach 1,000 drones a month and eventually increase to 3,000 drones a month.

“We’re building out [this capacity] specifically for Ukrainian demand, and as a company, we’re very invested in defense for Ukraine,” Enders said. “But we want to invest in European sovereignty, so [we] have built our [facility] in Germany for a time when the war in Ukraine ends, but we still want to show we have these capacities at home.”

Still, as the war continues unabated, drone development on both sides continues. Earlier this year, Ukraine’s Minister of Defense, Mykhailo Fedorov, requested that interceptor drone manufacturers develop and scale jet-powered interceptor drone systems to counter an influx of jet-powered, Shahed-type drones from Russia.

But the interceptor drones are working. According to the Ukraine Ministry of Defense, on March 24, 999 drones—including Shahed, Geran, and Gerbera-type decoy drones—were launched across Ukraine in 24 hours. More than 94% of these drones were intercepted.

“Drones have been a very good strategy for Russia, because for a long time, it completely exhausted Ukraine’s missile stocks,” Tollast said. “But as the Ukrainians get much better at consistently intercepting 90% of these drones, that strategy is starting to look shaky.”

Brave1’s Hrytseniuk would agree. Highlighting how the Ukraine Ministry of Defense also reported destroying a record 33,000-plus enemy UAVs in March, he said: “33,000—it’s unbelievable. That’s more than 1,000 a day.

“Russia is sending Shahed[-type] drones to Ukraine every day, and we destroy the majority of them,” he added. “This isn’t a war about humans fighting humans; it’s drones fighting drones. This is the new philosophy of our world.”

See also:

Drones with Edge AI: The Future of Warfare?

Swarming Drones: Scaling the Future of Warfare

data.title: SNUG India 2026: Synopsys Unveils First Multiphysics Fusion Tools Since Ansys Deal
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

At SNUG India 2026 last week, one year after completing its acquisition of Ansys, Synopsys released its first Multiphysics Fusion tools, combining EDA and physics analysis technologies in a single design flow for advanced semiconductor systems.

The portfolio combines Synopsys’ design tools and Ansys’ analysis technologies across timing signoff, design closure, multi-die design, and analog workflows.

For timing signoff, Synopsys has combined PrimeTime, RedHawk-SC, RedHawk-SC Electrothermal, StarRC, and HFSS-IC to include power, thermal, and stress effects in timing analysis. The company said the flow can reduce runtime and account for physical effects that may influence timing results.

For design closure, Synopsys has linked PrimeClosure with RedHawk-SC to incorporate power integrity analysis into signoff optimization. The company said the flow can reduce the number of design iterations required before signoff.

For multi-die designs, Synopsys has integrated its 3DIC Compiler platform with RedHawk-SC, RedHawk-SC Electrothermal, and HFSS-IC. The flow allows engineers to analyze power, thermal, and electromagnetic effects throughout the design process.

The portfolio also includes tools for analog and photonic design. Synopsys has integrated Custom Compiler with HFSS-IC for electromagnetic analysis and linked OptoCompiler with Lumerical for photonic IC and co-packaged optics development.

At SNUG India 2026, EE Times spoke to Ravi Subramanian, chief product management officer, and Sudeep Shivalli, regional senior director, GTM, Synopsys India, about the new portfolio, advanced node design challenges, and the impact of the Ansys acquisition.

Watch the full interview below:

Subramanian said the four joint solutions announced earlier this year at the company’s Converge conference were the result of six to eight months of collaboration between Synopsys and Ansys engineering teams following the acquisition.

The newly released products represent the first commercially available outcomes of that effort. Customers including Nvidia, Cisco, Samsung, and SiEnChips worked with Synopsys to validate the technologies using real designs and production technology nodes.

Subramanian said the primary benefit of the new solutions is the ability to analyze multiple engineering domains simultaneously rather than separately.

“In one sentence, the benefit is that instead of being overly conservative by analyzing each engineering domain separately, designers can jointly analyze those domains and dramatically reduce excess design margins,” he said.

The launch comes as semiconductor design complexity continues to increase at advanced process nodes and in multi-die architectures.

Advanced node technologies

Discussing Synopsys’ collaboration with TSMC on A14 and NanoFlex technologies, Subramanian said advanced node development requires close cooperation beginning at the transistor physics level. “TSMC wants the EDA tools to be ready as early as possible,” he said.

Shivalli told EE Times that the shift to advanced nodes has fundamentally changed design methodologies. “While older processes primarily focused on functionality, timing, and power optimization, advanced nodes introduce thermal effects, radiation effects, and other physical phenomena that must be tracked throughout the design cycle,” he said.

As TSMC develops new process technologies, Synopsys works alongside the foundry to build models, validate design flows, and prepare IP and EDA tools for customers. The collaboration extends from transistor physics through circuit, subsystem, and full-chip implementation.

“We create test cases together, run them through the tools, share the results, and work closely together,” Subramanian said. ”It is a very symbiotic process.”

The Ansys acquisition is also changing how Synopsys approaches advanced node and multi-die design. Subramanian pointed out that Synopsys and Ansys had already collaborated for nearly a decade before the acquisition, initially focusing on power integrity, IR-drop, and electromagnetic analysis.

However, the rise of 2.5D and 3D integration is making previously secondary effects central to chip design. Wires tend to behave like antennas as frequencies increase, creating electromagnetic interactions that affect signal integrity. Thermal behavior, mechanical stress, and package warpage can also directly affect electrical performance in these cases. “Effects that used to be considered second-order or third-order effects are now becoming first-order effects,” he said.

Subramanian pointed to the growing power demands of AI infrastructure as an example. While a mobile phone processor may consume approximately 8 W to 10 W, modern data-center processors can consume around 4 kW. “That is why we talk about all-aware signoff,” he said. “You must consider electrical, thermal, mechanical, and electromagnetic effects together in order to sign off a chip.”

The shift to advanced nodes has also increased the number of design vectors that engineers must evaluate. “That complexity also affects EDA software requirements and pricing. For legacy-node development, the software is relatively less expensive because certain advanced features are not required,” Shivalli said.

Navigating Indian and global markets

The integration of Ansys technologies is also extending into the Indian government-supported semiconductor startup initiatives. Asked whether Ansys software would be added to the programs that currently provide access to Synopsys tools, both executives confirmed that the process is already underway.

“We have already added some technologies that support chip design activities,” Shivalli said. “Our ambition in India is to help re-engineer engineering across multiple domains.”

Shivalli pointed to software-defined vehicles (SDVs) and digital twin technologies as areas where Indian OEMs could benefit from increased adoption of simulation-driven development.

The executives also highlighted growing opportunities beyond traditional semiconductor markets. According to Shivalli, government incentives in India over the past three years have expanded activity across approximately 16 to 17 sectors, including railways, shipbuilding, and intelligent electronic systems.

Subramanian described the global semiconductor industry as “a tale of two markets,” consisting of traditional segments such as mobile, automotive, industrial, and consumer electronics, as well as hyperscalers and AI infrastructure providers building increasingly large and complex chips. “We have been orienting our growth strategy around the challenges in these markets,” he said.

The Ansys acquisition is also strengthening Synopsys’ collaboration with Nvidia. Subramanian said both companies share a common vision centered on digital twins and virtualization. By combining accelerated simulation tools with Nvidia’s Omniverse platform, engineering teams can evaluate more design scenarios in virtual environments and reduce development cycles.

“The acquisition of Ansys has not changed our reliance on specific players,” he said. “However, after the Ansys acquisition, we expanded our collaboration with Nvidia.”

Tracking the AI trajectory

Subramanian believes AI agents have already arrived in enterprise environments, but the next phase of AI adoption will be driven as much by economics as by technical capability.

He said, “The cost of AI is energy. The cost of a token is directly related to the energy required to deliver that token.” As enterprises gain greater visibility into token usage and operating costs, they will increasingly compare the cost of AI-driven workflows against human labor and alternative approaches.

Subramanian said he believes business innovation built on top of AI economics could become as important as advances in AI models and agents themselves.

Concurrently, the company is advocating an open and secure agentic AI stack for the next stage of AI-driven engineering. Subramanian indicated that most semiconductor companies use Synopsys tools alongside products from other vendors and increasingly want AI agents from different suppliers to work together. Customers also want to develop their own agents while maintaining control over their data and AI models.

“Customers want openness because they need different tools and agents to work together,” he said. “They want security because they need their data protected. They want the learnings from their models protected as well.”

According to Subramanian, the move toward system-level simulation will not diminish the importance of chip design tools within Synopsys. Instead, he expects multi-die architectures and 3DIC designs to spur new categories of EDA tools and workflows.

“Every category of EDA tools is going to change,” he said.

The discussion also touched on open-source EDA initiatives emerging in India. Subramanian welcomed increased EDA research, arguing that the industry needs substantially more innovation than it currently produces. However, he noted that customers designing advanced node chips remain cautious about relying entirely on open-source tools because foundries require extensive validation and certification before production.

Shivalli added that adoption challenges extend beyond technology. Even legacy node semiconductor products can require investments of tens of millions of dollars before reaching silicon, making risk management a major concern for startups and customers alike.

Rather than focusing solely on open-source software, Shivalli said he believes broader access to cloud-based EDA, emulation, and semiconductor IP could have a greater impact on reducing barriers to entry.

“When we think about how to make innovation more accessible, we see the biggest opportunity in creating new ways of delivering access and making the entry point into chip design much easier, particularly in India,” he said.

data.title: CEA-Leti CEO: AI’s Real Bottleneck Is Architecture
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Speaking in an exclusive interview with EE Times ahead of Leti Innovation Days 2026 in Grenoble, CEA-Leti CEO Sébastien Dauvé argued that while the next phase of AI will be constrained by energy availability, the first challenge is how to integrate memory, photonics, communications, and sensing into increasingly complex systems.

“The bottleneck is really architectural,” he said. As AI workloads grow in scale and complexity, the challenge is increasingly about moving, storing, and managing data efficiently rather than simply adding more compute.

The theme echoes throughout this year’s Leti Innovation Days agenda, which covers topics ranging from advanced packaging and optical interconnects to memory-centric architectures and Europe’s semiconductor strategy.

AI moves into the physical world

According to Dauvé, the past year has marked a turning point for AI. “What has changed is the scale and speed of investment worldwide, across the entire value chain—compute, memory, photonics, packaging,” he said. “We now clearly see that AI is moving beyond experimentation into real applications.”

While much of the semiconductor industry has recently focused on training and inference inside large data centers, Dauvé sees a second wave coming. “We also see the emergence of physical AI,” he said.

Unlike generative AI systems confined to data centers, physical AI interacts directly with the real world through vehicles, robots, industrial equipment, healthcare devices, sensors, and other connected systems. That changes semiconductor design priorities, pushing intelligence closer to where data is generated and creating demand for more specialized, energy-efficient architectures.

These systems operate under strict constraints on power consumption, latency, reliability, and safety. They also require processing closer to where data is generated, driving demand for edge computing and increasingly specialized architectures.

That shift is reflected throughout the Leti Innovation Days agenda, which highlights applications spanning automotive systems, healthcare, defense, industrial automation, communications infrastructure, and next-generation sensing technologies. In Dauvé’s view, these emerging markets will increasingly shape semiconductor roadmaps alongside traditional data center requirements.

The bottleneck is architectural

For decades, semiconductor progress was driven primarily by transistor scaling. While process technology remains important, Dauvé said he believes future gains will increasingly come from how systems are organized and integrated. “There is no single technology,” he said. “It’s really about combining several building blocks.”

Those building blocks include FD-SOI platforms, advanced memories, silicon photonics, optical interconnects, advanced packaging, and heterogeneous integration. “The key point is integration,” Dauvé said. “We need to bring compute, memory, communication, and sensing together.”

The conference program reinforces that view. Sessions throughout the event focus on memory-bandwidth-centric computing, advanced 3D integration, chiplet-based architectures, optical-electronic convergence, and new approaches to system design. The common thread is a growing recognition that moving data efficiently has become as important as processing it.

Memory sits at the center of that discussion. Increasingly, the industry is treating memory not as a supporting component but as a primary determinant of AI system performance.

Dauvé argued that conventional architectures, which separate memory and compute into distinct domains, are becoming increasingly inefficient for AI workloads. “There is a lot of innovation in new types of memory—spintronics, FeRAM, and others,” he said. “But more importantly, memory and compute must be more tightly coupled.”

Bringing the two closer together reduces the energy and latency penalties associated with moving data across increasingly large AI systems.

Optical technologies are also becoming increasingly important. As AI clusters grow larger and more distributed, electrical interconnects face mounting challenges in bandwidth, latency, and energy consumption. Reflecting that trend, Leti Innovation Days will devote an entire track to optical interconnects, examining how silicon photonics could improve data movement both within and between future AI systems.

Together, these developments point toward a broader architectural shift in which memory, communication, and compute are increasingly co-designed rather than optimized independently.

Energy becomes the constraint

Architecture is only part of the challenge. The other is energy. According to Dauvé, industry must push digital technologies forward while accounting for environmental constraints, particularly energy consumption and materials use. “Data centers consume a huge amount of energy, and it is increasing rapidly,” he said.

Yet Dauvé argued that the challenge extends beyond improving chip efficiency. “The real issue is the availability of reliable electricity,” he said. “In some regions, projects are already being delayed—or even canceled—because the energy infrastructure cannot support them.”

The implication is that future AI growth may depend not only on semiconductor innovation but also on power generation, grid capacity, and infrastructure investment.

Many sessions at Leti Innovation Days address this challenge directly. Topics range from energy-efficient AI factories and sustainable semiconductor manufacturing to optical interconnects designed to reduce data-center power consumption. The RESOLVE initiative, which will be presented during the conference, is specifically examining how Europe can address what organizers describe as a “1,000× energy challenge” while maintaining competitiveness.

For Dauvé, solving these problems requires a system-level approach. “It means we cannot design technology in isolation anymore,” he said. “We need to co-design the entire stack—materials, devices, circuits, systems, and applications.”

The growing complexity of AI infrastructure also increases the need for collaboration. “The complexity of modern systems forces us to work across disciplines,” Dauvé said. “That’s why collaboration is essential.”

From research breakthroughs to industrial ecosystems

While much of the discussion around AI focuses on technology, Dauvé believes Europe faces a broader challenge: turning research leadership into industrial impact.

The emphasis on industrialization is reflected across initiatives such as FAMES and RESOLVE, which aim to strengthen Europe’s ability to move semiconductor innovations from research into production. The goal is not simply to invent new technologies, but to accelerate their path into production.

“We work not only on technology but also very closely with industrial partners and end users,” Dauvé said. “This allows us to connect research with real applications and industrial deployment.”

The same thinking shapes Dauvé’s view of Europe’s semiconductor strategy. “The first Chips Act was very positive,” he said. “It created strong momentum in terms of investment, collaboration, and engagement with industry.”

However, he said the next phase should focus more heavily on applications and markets. He also argued that Europe must strengthen capabilities across the entire value chain, including advanced packaging and heterogeneous integration. That objective is reflected in his vision for the end of the decade. “For me, success would be to have built a strong European value chain around advanced 3D integration and packaging,” Dauvé said.

Such an ecosystem would extend beyond packaging itself to include photonics, heterogeneous integration, manufacturing capabilities, and collaboration across research organizations and industry. “It does not have to be entirely European,” he said. “But it should be a strategic asset for Europe.”

In Dauvé’s view, the future of AI will not be defined by a single breakthrough device or process technology. Instead, it will depend on the industry’s ability to rethink architectures, manage energy constraints, integrate previously separate technologies, and accelerate the journey from laboratory innovation to industrial deployment.

For Dauvé, success by 2030 will not be measured by a single breakthrough device or process node. It will be measured by whether Europe succeeds in building a competitive ecosystem around advanced packaging, photonics, memory, and heterogeneous integration. In the AI era, he argued, the real challenge is no longer inventing individual technologies but integrating them into systems that can scale from laboratory breakthroughs to global deployment.

data.title: GlobalFoundries, Qualinx Put Europe’s Chip Sovereignty to the Fab Test
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Europe’s semiconductor sovereignty ambitions have often been measured in policy documents, subsidies, and fab announcements. GlobalFoundries and Qualinx now point to something more concrete: a security-critical chip that moved through a European manufacturing flow without sensitive design data or physical materials leaving the European Union.

The companies announced that Qualinx’s QLX3xx GNSS SoC has completed what they call the first fully European, end-to-end semiconductor manufacturing flow at GlobalFoundries’ Dresden fab. Built on GlobalFoundries’ FDX technology, it targets secure positioning, navigation, and timing applications in aerospace, defense, and critical infrastructure.

But the milestone requires precision. In an exclusive interview with EE Times, Qualinx CEO Tom Trill and Manfred Horstmann, general manager of GlobalFoundries EMEA, made clear that Europe has not achieved full semiconductor autonomy. What GlobalFoundries and Qualinx say they have demonstrated is narrower and more practical: a trusted route for chips whose data, masks, and wafers cannot move freely through global supply chains.

“The way I think about this is that what GlobalFoundries has done in Dresden is create the opportunity to have a full onshore supply chain,” Trill said. “That’s different from a fully onshore design chain.”

“The challenge of getting a full onshore design chain is very, very different from an onshore supply chain because there are near monopolies or duopolies in EDA, and the switching costs are very, very prohibitive,” Trill said. “So, the full onshore supply chain capability is now proven. But the design chain needs attention.”

Trill stressed that his point was not about anti-competitive behavior. Qualinx, he said, is treated fairly by its EDA suppliers and receives strong support. The issue is structural: EDA is a highly concentrated global market, and once a design flow is built around established tools, the cost and disruption of switching can make European alternatives difficult to adopt, even when sovereignty concerns make them strategically attractive.

A sovereign flow, not a sovereign stack

GlobalFoundries and Qualinx are not claiming Europe can now make every tool, material, IP block, or design software package needed for advanced semiconductors. They are claiming Europe can create a controlled lane where data residency, traceability, and trust are engineered into the process.

Horstmann described the flow as the result of a European footprint GlobalFoundries has built around Dresden. The process begins when a customer such as Qualinx provides the GDS file—the final digital blueprint describing the chip’s physical layout. GlobalFoundries then uses that data to generate the masks used in wafer production.

“Once they submit this GDS to GlobalFoundries, it’s completely in European hands,” he said. “It will stay on our own Europe-based servers.”

That does not mean every element of the broader semiconductor ecosystem is European. EDA remains the clearest example. Horstmann compared design software to Microsoft Word: The program may come from an external vendor, but the document belongs to the user and can still be protected. That distinction matters because chip design relies on a small group of dominant EDA suppliers, led by U.S.-based Synopsys and Cadence, with Siemens EDA the European-owned exception.

The same logic applies on the fab floor. GlobalFoundries’ Dresden site uses European equipment, including ASML lithography tools, but it also relies on systems from non-European suppliers. The point of the trusted flow is not to create a Europe-only tool chain. It’s to ensure that the sensitive design data, mask handling, and wafer processing remain under European control.

Why GNSS makes a strong test case

Qualinx was not originally looking for a sovereign flow. Trill said the decision to use GlobalFoundries was made before Covid-19 and before supply chain shocks pushed resilience and sovereignty to the top of Europe’s policy agenda.

“The decision to go with GlobalFoundries was made even before Covid,” Trill said. “So even the supply chain meltdown during Covid didn’t impact the decision because it was made beforehand.”

The fit, he said, was technical. Qualinx develops a digital RF platform capable of receiving signals from about 20 MHz to 8 GHz. Its first products target IoT and GNSS, including an analog front end and a full GNSS SoC. The company digitizes much of the analog RF function into CMOS.

“Fundamentally, we take 90% of analog RF and digitize it into the CMOS process,” Trill said. “We’re taking RF back in line with Moore’s Law, essentially.”

That makes GlobalFoundries’ 22FDX platform important to Qualinx’s roadmap. Trill said competitors are reaching scaling limits as they move RF products across nodes, forcing tradeoffs in die size, cost, or digital functionality. Qualinx expects to benefit from CMOS scaling over multiple generations.

The security implications make it a strong launch case. GNSS chips do more than provide location. They provide positioning, velocity, and timing data that critical systems depend on.

Trill pointed to Galileo as one example of the scale and strategic importance of GNSS. He said the European constellation had 4.1 billion connections last year from 32 operational satellites, with services including authentication, high precision, and emergency capabilities.

Qualinx’s message to customers, he said, is “designed and secured in Europe and available globally.”

For GlobalFoundries, the story is not only geographic. Horstmann said 22FDX is well-suited to the Qualinx application because it offers low-power consumption, strong RF and millimeter-wave performance, radiation hardness, and resistance to electromagnetic pulses.

That points to a broader issue in Europe’s semiconductor strategy. Sovereignty is often discussed through leading-edge logic. But many security-critical applications require trusted supply, low power, RF capability, long product lifetimes, and reliability more than the most advanced digital node.

The secure flow adds controls around data, masks, personnel access, and system separation. GlobalFoundries is also separating servers used for secure flows from standard commercial flows. Some steps are still manual, but GlobalFoundries aims to automate more of the secure tape-out process over the next two years.

Deutsche Telekom is part of that effort. Horstmann said GlobalFoundries brought in the European telecom operator to help assess whether its systems are suitable for restricted and classified data handling and to identify gaps.

Chips Act funding meets manufacturing reality

The announcement also gives Europe a concrete way to judge industrial-policy spending. Horstmann linked the effort to GlobalFoundries’ Dresden SPRINT investment and Chips Act funding. He said demand for 22FDX is high.

“We are permanently oversubscribed,” he said. “We are building out SPRINT, which adds capacity—an additional 150,000 wafers per year dedicated to exactly these kinds of applications.”

For Trill, the investment matters because it changes the economics of onshore manufacturing. “The investment [that] GlobalFoundries has made [makes] it economical at scale for us to onshore the production and manufacturing pieces,” he said.

That does not solve the rest of the value chain. Trill said the deeper problem is not always the absence of European capability. It is often the absence of capability at usable cost and scale. In one earlier case, Qualinx tried to onshore verification work and found it would cost seven times more in Europe.

Horstmann also pointed to gaps in materials. GlobalFoundries maps where materials come from and keeps alternatives for each one, he said, but many are not sourced in Europe. Rare earth materials and helium remain challenges.

The GlobalFoundries-Qualinx flow does not make Europe semiconductor-autonomous. It does something more limited—and potentially more useful. It shows how sovereignty can be engineered into manufacturing for chips inside critical systems.

If GlobalFoundries opens the trusted European flow to additional customers in 2027, the significance will not be that Europe has escaped the global semiconductor supply chain. It will be that Europe has learned how to build secure lanes through it.

How GlobalFoundries Takes AI from Pilot to Global Scale

GlobalFoundries: First Chipmaker to Support Open Standard for AI Scale Up

data.title: Defense Sends Clear Signal to Canadian Semiconductor Industry
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Canada’s defense and semiconductor ecosystem is at an inflection point: New policy signals are beginning to reshape how industry invests, sources chips, and protects intellectual property.

This shift is placing greater focus on sovereign capability and supply chain resilience, and increasing pressure to build more of the country’s critical technology stack at home.

At a recent CHIPS NORTH Executive Summit panel on defense and dual use, Joe Armstrong, president of 49North, a defense subsidiary of MDA Space, said the federal government’s Defense Industrial Strategy (DIS) is creating clearer demand signals for industry. “It gives industry a little more confidence.”

Wendy Hadwen, assistant deputy minister at the Canadian Department of National Defense (DND), said the DIS is not the finish line, but “the start line” to align federal programs, regulations, finance, and science around a clear defense demand signal.

She said Canada already has the raw ingredients to succeed: entrepreneurs, precision manufacturers, raw materials, an educated workforce, and a strong financial system. “There is no reason why any of those things are not positioned to win if they would be oriented to a defense demand signal.”

Hadwen also said the DIS depends on a major cultural shift inside government, where defense asks industry to innovate earlier and more directly, and warned that Canada cannot afford to delay building operational capacity. “In this space, we have no time to lose,” she said.

Sonya Shorey, president and CEO of Invest Ottawa, said the old divide between security policy and industrial strategy has disappeared. Her organization is focused on working with government, industry, academia, and investors to measure the Ottawa region’s impact and identify where it can scale fastest.

She highlighted the recent spinout of the Canadian Photonics Fabrication Center (CPFC) into a standalone compound semiconductor fab, which underscores the national capital region’s semiconductor and photonics strengths. “It is going to increase our sovereignty and security.”

Shorey pointed to Ottawa’s dense concentration of assets—including defense companies, sovereign institutions, embassies, and a large tech workforce—as a key reason the region can move quickly on national priorities. She also highlighted Ottawa’s role in supporting test infrastructure, procurement pathways, and matchmaking between startups, larger firms, and government buyers.

Armstrong pointed to a broader shift in buying behavior, with Canadian buyers and prime contractors showing greater interest in domestic suppliers capable of reliably meeting requirements. “I will always pick a Canadian supplier if we can meet the requirement that we need 100% of the time.”

He said the opportunity for Canada’s semiconductor industry is not just getting projects funded, but to turn that funding into durable industrial capacity, stronger sovereignty, and exportable Canadian capability.

Fabrication capacity, along with design and IP control, are both key contributors to Canada’s sovereignty, Armstrong said.

Panel moderator Duncan Stewart, partner at the Business Development Bank of Canada’s technology venture fund, said Canada is entering a “once in a 50-year opportunity” as new defense spending creates room for semiconductor and deep-tech growth. He said the country has long missed the link between national security and technology investment, but that is now changing.

Stewart said Canada must treat semiconductors as central to sovereignty and defense—they are not just components, but the foundation of systems used across defense and intelligence. “Semiconductors are core to every single system that operates in the world today,” he said.

While the future of Canada hinges on its ability to turn its science into technology and then turn that into money, Stewart said defense markets require patience, communication, and system-level thinking.

Rafal Janek, COO of Xanadu, cautioned that there is still a gap between what startups can offer and what defense customers understand they need. Canadian firms often have the underlying IP, but not always a product packaged for immediate procurement, which can slow adoption.

Janik said the pandemic exposed how vulnerable Canada is to semiconductor disruptions, and he argued that the country needs to become “indispensable” in at least one part of the global ecosystem. He said that means identifying a niche Canada can build better than anyone else, so it can remain relevant when the next geopolitical shock hits.

Janik also cautioned that startup partnerships with overseas manufacturers can carry IP and leverage risks, especially when smaller firms need access to large fabs.

As a quantum company, Xanadu is being pulled into a broader defense-driven investment cycle, as are many photonic companies, Janik said.

He said the past five years have brought a major change in how quantum is funded globally, with defense agencies increasingly shaping the market rather than pure economic-development programs, pointing to the initiatives such as the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) Quantum Benchmarking Initiative (QBI), of which Xanadu is a participant, and Canada’s own Quantum Champions program.

Janik said these investments often look like quantum programs on the surface but ultimately strengthen the semiconductor supply chain more broadly. “The money ends up building capabilities that are ubiquitous for many other semiconductor applications,” he said.

data.title: Amazon’s Newest Gambit: Selling AI Chips
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Nvidia’s $5 trillion chip empire apparently has a new challenge: Two of its largest customers are planning to sell their in-house AI chips directly to data center companies. In April this year, Google unveiled plans to sell its custom tensor processing units (TPUs) to select customers, and now Amazon is in talks to sell its Trainium AI chips directly to other companies for use in their data centers.

In fact, Amazon CEO Andy Jassy signaled this move back in April in a letter to shareholders. “It is quite possible Amazon would sell racks of its chips to third parties,” he wrote. He also noted that the company’s in-house chip business had surpassed a $20 billion annual revenue run rate.

Amazon’s semiconductor portfolio comprises Graviton CPUs, Trainium and Inferentia AI accelerators, and Nitro networking cards. Graviton is an Arm-based CPU that provides a low-power alternative to traditional server workhorses from Intel and AMD.

Trainium is a purpose-built AI accelerator designed to train and run massive machine learning models. On the other hand, Inferentia chips are custom-designed, purpose-built processors specifically optimized for machine learning inference. Amazon claims Inferentia AI chips are 40% cheaper to run for generating responses from AI models.

There are reports that Uber is among the first external partners to adopt the Trainium3 AI chip. Then, there is Anthropic, which has committed to deploying over one million Trainium chips in its data centers hosted by Amazon Web Services (AWS). It’s worth noting that Amazon has invested $4 billion in this startup, best known for its Claude AI assistant. In return, Anthropic declared AWS as its primary cloud and training partner.

But Amazon’s chip business is more of an enigma: A $20 billion semiconductor business no one talks about. It also mirrors a semiconductor business model pioneered by Apple and Google, which developed and deployed custom chips in their hardware at various scales. Below is a sneak peek at Amazon’s semiconductor journey that began in 2015 with the acquisition of Annapurna Labs.

Amazon’s chip trajectory

Amazon’s $350 million acquisition of Annapurna Labs set the foundation for its bespoke silicon solutions. With this in-house semiconductor expertise, the company began developing custom AI silicon around 2019. A year later, Amazon unveiled the Trainium AI accelerator. Trainium1 was a modest chip primarily designed to handle inference workloads within AWS.

Trainium2, launched in 2024, aimed to deliver 4× the performance and 3× the memory capacity of its precursor, Trainium1. It incorporated advanced features such as enhanced heat management and fewer internal components, which boosted its compute performance. These improvements were especially instrumental in training AI models.

Trainium3, launched in late 2025, claims to deliver up to 4× the performance of its predecessor, Trainium2. It’s a 3-nm AI accelerator designed for training and running large-scale generative AI models in the cloud. The third version of the Trainium chip is largely sold out, according to Amazon AI chief Peter DeSantis.

Annapurna Labs in Austin has also helped develop several generations of Graviton; Amazon has recently started shipping this general-purpose processor to Meta.

An Nvidia challenger?

Are Amazon’s chip ambitions a side hustle or a serious challenge to Nvidia? Trade media is abuzz with commentaries that see the sale of custom chips from Amazon and Google as a competitive threat to Nvidia. Especially when Amazon and Google have been developing alternatives to Nvidia’s GPUs. However, it’s important to note that Nvidia is selling a full stack, not just GPUs.

So, while custom AI accelerators from Amazon and Google are likely to win in specific workloads and cost optimization, Nvidia will continue to dominate general-purpose AI infrastructure. Nvidia’s edge isn’t merely in silicon; it also lies in CUDA, developer tooling, partnerships, and support. That’s why Amazon is developing the AWS Neuron—its AI model development platform—around Trainium chips.

But building hardware, software, and support ecosystems around silicon requires an enormous effort and significant time. And besides Nvidia’s established ecosystem, the bespoke nature of Amazon and Google’s AI chips may also hinder rapid adoption.

So, for now, it seems less of a competitive threat to Nvidia and more of a “seize the moment” move to take advantage of an unprecedented demand for AI chips. However, the fact that nobody likes one company—Nvidia—to dominate the entire market could also help the chip ambitions of Amazon and Google along the way.

Read also:

Amazon on AI and Accelerators

AWS Rolls Out AI Inference Chip

The Trillion-Dollar Race to Fragment the Nvidia Monopoly

data.title: Billions Pour into Autonomous Defense as AI Redefines Warfare
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

In May, U.S. defense tech startup Anduril landed a massive $5 billion in funds, one of the largest private investments in defense history, to aggressively expand manufacturing and accelerate research and development of autonomous drones and other systems. As co-founder and CEO Brian Schimpf wrote in an investor letter: “When we founded Anduril in 2017, defense was not a category that attracted significant venture investment. That has changed meaningfully over the last several years.”

Schimpf’s words are apt. Geopolitical instability, shifting national security priorities, and rapid technological progress have driven a surge in both public and private aerospace and defense spending. U.S. analyst S&P Global reports that worldwide private equity and VC investment reached $10.63 billion in 2025, more than double that of 2024’s total. Meanwhile, the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute says that global public defense spending rose to a record $2.89 trillion in 2025.

As the investment floods in, companies developing autonomous hardware and software are winning big time. Anduril aside, in 2026 so far, U.S.-based Shield AI raised $1.5 billion to accelerate the development of surveillance drones, and U.S.-based True Anomaly secured $650 million to advance autonomous orbital vehicles and fly multiple missions. Harmattan AI of France also received $200 million to scale the development and manufacturing of AI-enabled intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR) drones, drone-interception systems, and electronic warfare platforms.

Echoing wider industry sentiment, Shield AI said: “[Our] investments reflect a fundamental shift in how defense capabilities are built: increasingly shaped in software, trained in simulation, and improved through use.”

As Anduril’s Schimpf also highlighted in his investor letter: “The convergence of technological possibility and geopolitical instability is the defining strategic reality of our time. … Our focus is on range, mass, and coordination. Autonomous systems are an enabler—not the objective.”

Governments are also responding. In late 2025, the U.K. pledged more than £140 million (~$188 million) for drones and anti-drone weapons, following a £4 billion (~$5.4 billion) funding package for autonomous systems, while Germany’s Bundeswehr budget has reached an all-time high of more than €108 billion (~$125 billion) amid orders for thousands of autonomous combat drones. Industry reporting also indicates that the U.S. Department of Defense has asked Congress for $54 billion to fund autonomous drone warfare initiatives.

Alessandro Pianelli, aerospace and defense consultant, photonics optical engineer, and author of the recently released white paper, “Aerospace & Defence: 30 Year Outlook 2026-2056,” has been tracking industry developments with interest. Highlighting how the entire sector is “embarking on an unprecedented phase of transformation,” he noted that AI-enhanced autonomous systems are already reshaping modern military operations.

As he highlighted in his report, recent deployments of nearly 10,000 AI-enabled drones, mostly in Ukraine, have achieved strike accuracies 3× to 4× higher than human-piloted systems. “These drones, ranging from modified consumer models to advanced systems, produced by companies such as Anduril, Shield AI, and Helsing [of Germany], can navigate, identify targets, and operate even when traditional communications links are disrupted by electronic warfare,” he wrote. “What we’ve seen happen in Ukraine is now expanding everywhere.”

As industry pushes for autonomy, including under GPS- and communications-denied conditions, future defense operations will increasingly depend on software-defined networks, sensor fusion, multi-agent coordination, and adaptive spectrum management. Pianelli noted how recent advances in foundation models and edge AI are already advancing autonomy.

“Large, multimodal models are being adopted for ISR fusion, mission planning, and sensor interpretation under degraded communications,” he said. “[Meanwhile,] compact AI accelerators now enable real-time, on-board inference at the edge.”

In one example, U.S.-based AnySignal has developed an end-to-end RF platform that detects jamming in real time and autonomously adapts to maintain mission communications. Having raised some $40 million in venture funds to develop its tech and scale production, the company has just inked a deal with the U.S. Space Force, the U.S. Armed Forces’ space services branch, to bolster its Military Satellite Communications network against electronic warfare.

Software for swarms

Looking to the future, Pianelli believes that autonomous swarms will play a central role in defense, with both large semi-autonomous and fully autonomous systems supporting surveillance, precision strike, and operations in denied environments by the mid-2030s. “The maturation of edge AI will embed adaptive decision-making directly into platforms, ranging from next-generation aircraft to unmanned systems,” he said.

Ukraine-founded Swarmer underscores the significance of this communal approach. Established in 2023, the company reports that its edge-AI-based swarm command-and-control platform has been validated across more than 100,000 combat missions. Swarmer became the first Ukrainian defense technology company to list on Nasdaq earlier this year and has just won a $2.8 million contract from Ukraine’s Meta Bureau to integrate its swarming software into the defense tech company’s drones. Other notable software platforms include Shield AI’s Hivemind, Palladyne AI’s SwarmOS, and Anduril’s Lattice, all pointing to the growing importance of drone swarms in future defense systems.

Taken together, these industry developments indicate that the defense sector will become increasingly reliant on software-defined autonomy. Schimpf amply summed this up in Anduril’s recent funding announcement: “Recent analysis suggests that improvements in autonomy will enable swarms far more powerful than any individual platform, with employment driven at costs once thought unimaginably low. … The convergence of artificial intelligence, autonomy, and advanced sensing is reshaping warfare.”
